summary:
Joan of Arc (c. 1412–1431 AD) was a peasant girl from Lorraine who, claiming divine visions commanding her to drive the English from France, emerged as the charismatic military leader who turned the tide of the [[event:2235|Hundred Years' War]] and secured the coronation of Charles VII at Reims — one of the most extraordinary careers in medieval history and one of the most remarkable individual interventions in any major war. At seventeen, she persuaded the Dauphin Charles to give her command of French forces, raised the English siege of Orléans (May 1429), and led a series of rapid military victories that created the conditions for Charles's coronation, which legitimized his kingship in French eyes.

Joan's subsequent campaign was less successful, and she was captured by Burgundian forces in 1430 and sold to the English. Tried for heresy and witchcraft in a Church court controlled by English-aligned ecclesiastics in Rouen, she was condemned for refusing to recant her claim to divine guidance and burned at the stake on May 30, 1431 — at approximately nineteen years of age. A posthumous retrial in 1456 overturned the conviction, declaring the proceedings irregular. The Roman Catholic Church canonized her in 1920. Joan's life and death became a central element of French national mythology: the peasant girl who embodied France, martyred by England and treachery, victorious in God's cause. Her military contribution was real — her raising of the Orléans siege was a genuine strategic turning point — but her lasting significance was the symbolic and psychological inspiration she provided to French resistance at its lowest point.
